videos.title: Inside San Francisco’s Worst Block
videos.description:
We went to the world’s largest open-air drug market in San Francisco’s Tenderloin. There are a few thousands of people living on the streets there, and we went to find out how they got there and how people are trying to make it better.

This is an overview and I've done separate videos that go into each of these neighborhoods in depth regarding schools, costs, specific restaurants, all of that. So take some notes on the neighborhoods that interest you most and you can check out our vlogs, which will be in the description below. I've lived in the Bay Area for over 20 years and though I haven't lived in all of these neighborhoods, I've spent a lot of time in each of them because I've sold over a thousand homes and I've also gotten to hear the perspective from both buyers and sellers. This list is based on my experience and though I can't possibly fit 89 neighborhoods into five tiers, I'm going to do my best but I'm sure I'm going to be getting a lot of flack in the comments section so I'm sorry in advance. So starting with the worst at number five, I only have two neighborhoods on that list and if you open up any headlines about San Francisco, you're going to see a lot of stories about crime, homelessness, dirtiness, all of that. A lot of those headlines are honestly concentrated around two neighborhoods, the Civic Center and the Tenderloin. And to be honest, most residents, given the choice, are not stepping foot in those neighborhoods. Now let's move on to tier number four. For tier number four, I have the Inter Mission and SoMa. So Inter Mission actually has a lot going for it. If you look at what makes a neighborhood valuable in San Francisco, sun and walkability are top on the list and this neighborhood actually has both of those things. Inter Mission has some fantastic restaurants in it. Flower and Water is one of my favorite but there's honestly too many to mention. So actually I think there's a lot of long term potential in the neighborhood, but it has also been hit by some crime and some homelessness and is a little bit dirty right now. Soma is similar but different. It actually does have some crimes and homelessness, some dirtiness mixed in. There's actually a very different kind of architecture there. It's a lot of high rise architecture there. And I would say the restaurant scene is actually not as happening there. But if you live in these high rises, it can be really convenient living. You can have gyms, everything that you need, concierge service, 24-hour security right in your building and you don't really have to leave. Of course with those benefits, they do often come with high HOA dues and we actually have done separate videos on the top 10 things to avoid when buying a condo in San Francisco. Both neighborhoods have pockets that are quite nice. So if you're willing to hang in there for a little while and wait for the neighborhood to get cleaned up, you can really get your dollar to stretch a long way in those neighborhoods right now. I'm Ruth Krishnan and I'm founder of the Krishnan Team. My team and I have been selling real estate in San Francisco for over a decade. And if you have any questions during this video, feel free to text this number right here. We'd be happy to help out and we'd love to be your real estate team of choice for anything to do with real estate in San Francisco. So for my third tier, I'm going to add in some neighborhoods that are great for me, but they're lacking a few things. So one of them is Clarendon Heights. Clarendon Heights is made up primarily of single-family homes and they can be usually purchased between about three million and about six million dollars, but there's definitely some outliers out there. Next up, the Outer Richmond. The Outer Richmond has become quite the hotspot for homes under one point seven million dollars. Though you can score a home near the median price point, there's actually quite a few homes sprinkled in there between like the two and the three million dollar price point. Next, we have Bernal Heights, and Bernal Heights is a great neighborhood. I like it a lot. It was the first neighborhood that my husband and I ever lived in an apartment together. And to be honest, at the time, I didn't even know that there were neighborhoods south of Cesar Chavez. Fast forward 20 years and Bernal Heights has been written up numerous times as one of the hottest neighborhoods in San Francisco. It's got a very bohemian feel to it with a very community-centric vibe. The reason why it's on my third tier list and not my second is because I actually think Bernal should be broken up into four different neighborhoods. videos.description:
San Francisco, a city steeped in history, culture, and innovation, offers an unparalleled array of experiences for visitors and locals alike.

0:00 Introduction
0:56 GOLDEN GATE PARK
1:50 LOMBARD STREET
2:46 AQUARIUM OF THE BAY
4:08 PALACE OF FINE ARTS
4:44 CABLE CARS
5:37 TRANSAMERICA PYRAMID
6:24 ALCATRAZ
7:24 THE TWIN PEAKS
8:08 FISHERMAN'S WHARF
8:46 GOLDEN GATE BRIDGE

Known for its iconic landmarks, vibrant neighborhoods, and diverse attractions, San Francisco is a destination that captivates the senses and leaves lasting memories. Whether you're exploring the city for the first time or returning for another adventure, there are countless things to do in San Francisco that cater to every interest and preference.

For those seeking the best things to do in San Francisco, a visit to the Golden Gate Bridge is an absolute must. This engineering marvel, with its striking red hue, offers breathtaking views of the Pacific Ocean and the city skyline. Walking or biking across the bridge provides a unique perspective on this architectural wonder, making it one of the top things to do in San Francisco. Additionally, the nearby Muir Woods National Monument, with its towering redwoods, offers a serene escape into nature, perfect for a day trip.

If you're planning a weekend getaway, there are plenty of things to do in San Francisco this weekend to keep you entertained. From exploring the colorful murals of the Mission District to strolling through the Japanese Tea Garden in Golden Gate Park, the city is alive with cultural and recreational activities. For a more relaxed experience, consider a leisurely ride on the historic cable cars, which have been a staple of San Francisco's charm for over a century.

Families visiting the city will find plenty of fun things to do in San Francisco that cater to children. The San Francisco Zoo, with its wide variety of animals and interactive exhibits, is a favorite among kids. For a more educational outing, the California Academy of Sciences, home to the Steinhart Aquarium and the planetarium, offers a fascinating glimpse into the natural world. These activities make San Francisco an excellent destination for things to do in San Francisco with kids.

For those looking to experience the city's vibrant nightlife, there are plenty of cool things to do in San Francisco. The Fillmore Auditorium and the Warfield Theatre host world-class concerts, while the city's numerous bars and lounges provide a lively setting for socializing. A night tour of the Golden Gate Bridge, illuminated in stunning colors, is another unforgettable way to enjoy things to do in San Francisco at night.

Whether you're a first-time visitor or a seasoned traveler, San Francisco's dynamic energy and endless possibilities ensure that there's always something new to discover. With its rich history, stunning landscapes, and diverse cultural offerings, San Francisco remains one of the most enchanting cities in the world. Whether you're exploring the top 10 things to do in San Francisco or simply strolling through its iconic neighborhoods, this city is sure to leave a lasting impression.

financial heart of Northern California San Francisco is also one of America's great walking cities with lots of Pathways and cycling trails and so many interesting sites to see and explore this is the city that has everything making it a fantastic place to visit before we get into the top 10 things to do in San Francisco we've included links in the description to various discount codes and links to resources of things to do so make sure you check those out at number 10 is the Golden Gate Park once an area of sand dunes the Golden Gate Park is now home to Gardens and museums and is a fabulous green space in the heart of San Francisco at 17 Acres the park stretches for almost 50 city blocks from the middle of the city to the foot of the bridge some of the main attractions within the park include the D Young Museum the California Academy of Sciences Museum and the San Francisco Botanical Garden one of the most popular attractions is the Japanese tea garden with beautiful plants ponds Bridges and Japanese style structures including the Tea House the park also has an interesting network of walking trails and cycling paths several Lakes Bridge paths and even a buffalo padic Golden Gate Park is one of those places that can easily take a couple of hours or a couple of days bike rentals are available and this can be a good way to explore the park rather than trying to do everything on foot with so many areas to explore it's good to ensure that you have plenty of time for your visit number nine is Lombard Street San Francisco's most famous residential street has a popular reputation as the crookedest in the city lined with Victorian mansions and neat lawn landscaping Lombard Street is also considered one of San Francisco's most scenic streets the city is well known for its steep hills and this street is no exception with eight sharp hairpin turns in 180 M it would simply be too steep to tackle in a straight line one of the most popular things to do by visitors is to hike all the way up the curves and smell the beautiful flowers planted in the beds along the way but to get the full experience of snaking and breaking down the zigzagging road it is definitely more thrilling to drive down the oneway street from the top you can also ride a cable car up the hill and get Splendid views and then Traverse down by foot this iconic Street featured in many movies is also a popular spot for photographers as you can capture both the entire winding street from the bottom as well as the spectacular view from the top Additionally you can look out over the city landscape which includes the Bay Bridge and koit Tower at number eight is the Aquarium of the Bay discover the beauty and diversity of Northern Californian Aquatic Life at Aquarium of the Bay located on the historic San Francisco waterfront at Pier 39 the aquarium is home to more than 20,000 marine animals ranging from 8ft native of sharks to Rays jellies and even river otters all of which can be found in San Francisco Bay or along the California coastline with a focus on education conservation and entertainment the Aquarium of the Bay's exhibition space is organized as a series of experiences that invite visitors to both observe and touch the animals on display there are four main exhibit areas for guests to explore the first named discover the Bay highlights some of the animals that are commonly found closest to shore right off of San Francisco's docks under the bay is the aquarium's main and most iconic exhibit area and when visit first enter they step into a tranquil environment where they discover a mesmerizing jelly exhibit featuring moon jellies Pacific Sea Nettles and more touch the bay offers a hands-on experience four aquarium visitors and guests can touch juvenile bat rays and even sharks in the exhibit's first touch pool finally guests will see the aquarium's newest exhibit the river otters this exhibit features playful river otters in a Serene environment that is a combination of land and water habitats the exhibit includes two freshwater pools a river stream plus plenty of dry land and dig pits for the the otter to enjoy this amazing aquarium has a mission to protect the bay and the animals that live there and is well worth a visit number seven is the Palace of Fine Arts the Palace of Fine Arts is easily one of the most beautiful places to visit in all of San Francisco and is the last remaining structure from the 1915 Panama Pacific exhibition listed on the national register of historic places this classical looking building with a French inspired Dome structure is beautifully situated on a lagoon that reflects the mirror image on the surface of the calm water while ducks and geese drift by the palace has been restored along with the grounds and today hosts art exhibitions and performances the Palace of Fine Arts theater seats approximately 1,000 patrons and offers a variety of shows musicals and cultural events at number six are the cable cars cable cars were introduced in 1873 to help locals contend with the many Hills the city is built on today the few remaining cable cars offer visitors a great way to explore the city in historic fashion since 1964 these tram like vehicles have had the unique distinction of being the only public transport system to be declared a historic Monument the world famous cable cars run on three lines in the Steep Streets of San Francisco between Market Street and Fisherman's Warf these cars are a fun ride especially if you get to stand on the running board San Francisco is very proud of their cable car system as it is the last manually operated system in the world today at its peak there were 23 Cable Car lines in the city but today there is just three left the cable car system forms part of the urban transport Network operated by the San Francisco Municipal Railway and if you are planning on more than a couple of rides or are going to be sightseeing for a few days it's a good idea to buy a pass at number five is the Trans America pyramid big cities across the world are recognizable due to their unique architecture and distinguishable skylines and San Francisco is no exception though visitors can easily pick out the Trans America pyramid from photographs of San Francisco it's more than just another building the Trans America pyramid is a building that is famous for its futuristic architectural style located in the heart of the financial district the iconic pyramid is San Francisco's other famous symbol besides besides the Golden Gate Bridge finished in 1972 the Trans America Pyramid has a height of 260 M and is now the second tallest building of the San Francisco skyline this looming Landmark features a white quartz exterior and more than 3,000 Windows visitors can see the view from the 48th floor from 24-hour camera feeds displayed in the visitor center located on the Plaza level off of Clay Street which is well worth a look at number four is a visit to Alcatraz often referred to as the rock the small island of Alcatraz has served as a Lighthouse a military fortification and as a prison it was home to some of the most notorious criminals of all time including Al Capone and Machine Gun Kelly in the course of its 30-year existence the penitentiary received a total of 1,576 convicts there were never more than 250 at any one time even though there were 450 cells measuring about 10 ft by 4 ft at times the number of guards and staff were greater than the number of convicts surrounded by the swirling currents and freezing water of San Francisco Bay Alcatraz was believed to be inescapable since its in 1963 Alcatraz has become a national landmark operated by the National Park Service and welcomes over 1 million visitors a year you can take a fery over to the island and the informative pre-recorded audio tours are strongly recommended as they allow you to learn about the prison as you roam the grounds at your own pace as one of the most popular places to visit this amazing experience is definitely not one to be missed in San Francisco number three is the Twin Peaks the Twin Peaks in San Francisco are a world famous tourist attraction and offer spectacular views of the Bay area these famous grassy Hills rise 922 ft from the ground making them the second highest point in the city after Mount Davidson the two peaks are adjacent to each other and from the top visitors can view multiple San Francisco landmarks including the Bay Bridge and the downtown skyscrapers the Twin Peaks are open from 5:00 a.m. to midnight and there is free parking at the top of Twin Peaks Boulevard it is best to stay on the mark trails and dress for windy conditions if you are planning to hike to the top there are Pathways up to the top of both Peaks and they've recently been improved with rustic wooden stairs although there are are some relatively Steep ascents and descents on some of the trails the Fantastic views are definitely worth it at number two is Fisherman's Warf one of the most popular tourist areas in San Francisco Fisherman's Warf runs all the way from Pier 39 with the famous colony of cheeki sea lions through to Municipal Pier at the end of Aquatic Park the warf is rich in history and for over a century its historic Waterfront was once the Hub of San Francisco's fishing fleet it's now a fun place to stroll around and get a taste for the city with its beautiful setting along the water front a diverse number of restaurants are also available offering some of the freshest Seafood in the city with most caught just beyond the docks other tourist attractions at the warf include lots of museums souvenir stores historical buildings and fantastic Scenic views over the bay and the top spot is the famous Golden Gate Bridge the Golden Gate Bridge was the longest suspension bridge in the world when it was completed in 1937 measuring approximately 2 km in length it has become an internationally recognized symbol of San Francisco and a mustsee place place of interest the bridge took 4 years to build and spans the Golden Gate The Straits between San Francisco and Maron County to the north with its distinctive red metal work on sunny days it transfixes crowds with a radiant glow which is maintained by the painters that apply 1,000 gallons of red orange paint every week visitors can drive over the Golden Gate Bridge or use the walkways on either side of the bridge that are open to pedestrians and cyclists the walk begins at the start of the bridge accessible from the precidio shuttle and ends with a Viewpoint in Marin County at night the flood lit structure is equally striking but the bridge is only open to pedestrians and cyclists during daylight hours so that sums up our top 10 things to do in San Francisco we hope you enjoyed if you did please leave a like and if you're new here feel free to subscribe until next video have a great day

videos.transcript: Hi, this is Ryan and this is Manuel. And if you only have two days in San Francisco, these are our top 10 places to [Music] visit. We absolutely love San Francisco. It's lively, charming, historic, and it's got a mix of coastal and cosmopolitan vibes. and we cannot wait to share with you our top 10 places to go. First off is Alcatraz. Now, we've always wanted to go, so we booked our tickets through the Alcatraz city cruises website, which is the only official place where you can book your tickets. It was around $45 when we booked. You must arrive there 30 minutes before your departure time. There's a bag policy in place, but we all had backpacks and it was absolutely fine. There are toilets on the pier and also a nice model of Alcatras and its history. The journey to the island took approximately 10 minutes which was worth the ticket price alone as you get amazing views of San Francisco, the Golden Gate Bridge, and Alcatraz. The approach to the island was very exciting as straight away you could see just how much history there was to get lost in. If you're hungry, luckily there's a cafe on board that serves coffee, pastries, and many other things. And there are also restrooms. When we arrived, we disembarked and we were greeted by a ranger from the National Park Service. And that was very useful because it gave us lots of information about the place and it got us even more excited about our exploration. The main attraction on the island is the cell house self-guided audio tour that allows you to go inside one of the most infamous places in American history. Yeah, and this was no doubt the highlight of our Alcatra visit. We were there with my parents who don't speak English, but fortunately they provide the tour in at least 10 different languages. On the tour, you listen to the voices of former inmates and officers who really immerse you in the world of Alcatraz. You'll walk through the prison blocks and you'll hear stories of prison operations and daily routines. But for us, what was most exciting was to hear about the famous escape of 1962 when three men escaped using sharpened spoons to dig through their cells and then broke out through ventilation ducts into a utility corridor, leaving behind dummy heads made of plaster to fool the guards. They escaped, but they've never been seen again. So, this still remains an open investigation. And you can hear stories of famous inmates such as Machine Gun Kelly, Al Capone, and of course the Birdman of Alcatraz, who was there for 54 years. They were imprisoned in the hole, which held prisoners in solitary confinement, and this is where they were subject to harsh punishments. It was actually quite haunting. The self-guided tour is about half an hour, but you can pause and start when you want. The route is easy to follow, and you always know exactly where you're supposed to be going. You can't get lost. We even met William Baker, a former inmate who is now 91 years old. He was incarcerated in Alcatras for 3 years after two escape attempts from other prisons. He's often there to sign copies of his book, and he was very happy to chat to people and take pictures. After the tour, we started to explore outside of the cell house. One of the other reasons that we were looking forward to Alcatraz was to see what was left of the occupation of Alcatraz, where almost 100 Native Americans occupied the island for 19 months. You can still see remnants of this over the whole island which we found fascinating. While exploring the island, I have to say I really wasn't expecting to see all the wildlife and vegetation that we did see. We saw comands, herand sort of birds. There were even a few park rangers with binoculars to help you identify them. I thought that was so cool. Also outside you get amazing views of San Francisco and the Golden Gate Bridge. So, how long do you need for the tour? We'd say you'd probably need around 3 hours, which includes the boat ride to and from the island, the cell house audio tour, and some exploration. Pro tip, we booked the very first time slot to get to the island because we heard that it was the best way to get the island or to yourselves. And I have to say it really paid off because for about half an hour, we had the whole island all to ourselves, ourselves, and about 100 more people that were on the boat with us. The second of our must dos in San Francisco is Fisherman's Wararf, which is only a 10-minute walk from Pier 33. Yeah. And even if it's a very touristic place, the truth is that you cannot visit San Francisco without coming here. And we actually really enjoyed it. The first thing we did here was Pier 39, which is on two levels with dining, entertainment, shopping, and attractions, all surrounded by amazing views of the city and the bay. I love wildlife, and what I love most about Pier 39 was to see the sea lions. There were so many and they were so loud. Yes, that loud. They were just so fascinating to see. I could have spent hours there and Ryan had to drag me away. For lunch, we went to Bodin, which was a 10-minute walk from Pier 39 and is the oldest continually operating business in San Francisco. It's been there for more than 170 years. Bodin is famous for its sourdough bread, which is trademarked as the original San Francisco sourdough. It's a lovely place to eat. I ate the crab chowder and Manuel had the classic clam chowder. Both served in a sourdough bowl. They were delicious. Just seconds away is the famous fisherman's warf sign. The whole area has plenty of opportunities for shopping and dining, but it was time for a dessert. So, what better place to visit than the original Ghiradelli? Girodellis is only a few minutes walk away and its history spans back to over a century ago. Yeah, it was originally a chocolate factory established by Dominico Gueradelli, who was Italian, just like me. He's very proud of his Italian heritage. Yes, I am. When we went in, the smell was incredible. It's over two floors with a giant chocolate fountain connecting them. The ambiance is really cute. You can even sit inside a trolley car upstairs. We ordered one of their famous sundaes, which was big enough to share, and also ordered a coffee. It's a perfect pit stop if you're in need of a little pickme up. And when you're done, you can explore Gear Deli Square, which is home to other businesses and a beautiful fountain. Do you have some extra time? If you do, then this area is not short of things to do. You can see the old Canary, which is now a shopping area, and the San Francisco Maritime National Historic Park. We love maritime history, and we couldn't resist popping into the visitor center to see the exhibit. Also, from Giraadellis, we could see the old ships that are part of the National Historic Park. Well worth the visit. Stop number three on our list is Lombard Street, which is one of the most iconic roads in America. To get there, we walked only 15 minutes from Gear Delies via Hide Street. Because of our itinerary, we decided to get there reaching the top of it and then walk down. But beware, it's a steady uphill climb, but wow, the views were amazing. We could see Alcatraz, the Golden Gate, and we even had a few trolleys passing by. Lombar Street is claimed to be the crookedest street in the world and apparently over 2 million people visited each year. When we got to the top, we started the walk down the path. It was lovely and doesn't take long at all. However, it was very busy and there's actually not much to do here. So, we were ready to move on to the next place on our list. Stop number four on our list is Kite Tower, which is either a 25-minute walk from Lombard Street or a 5-minute drive. Ko Tower is named after Lily Ichko Koit, a wealthy patron of the city's firefighters. When she died in 1929, she left a substantial amount of money for, as she said, the purpose of adding to the beauty of the city I have always loved. You can go up the tower for a 360° view of the city and the bay for $10. But this wasn't available when we were there. So instead, we checked out the murals on ground level, which were incredible. The murals depict life in California during the depression and are well worth checking out. Even if you don't go up the tower, the views from Telegraph Hill are great. Only 5 minutes away from Ko Tower is North Beach. And if you're interested in the beat generation or anything Italian, this is the right place for you. The city's Little Italy offers some of San Francisco's most popular architectural landmarks, but it's also a perfect spot to wander beautiful streets and soak up the richly cultured neighborhood. For us, the highlight is City Lights Bookstore, which was founded in the 50s by Lawrence Ferlingetti and was famous for publishing beat generation writers and was the center of national attention when copies of Alan Ginsburg's Howl were seized by authorities and Feringetti was arrested and charged with selling obscene material. City Lights still publishes today and you absolutely cannot miss the poetry room upstairs. Just outside you can have a little stroll down Carrick Alley, a tribute to Big Generation writers. If you fancy a coffee stop, Vuvio Cafe is next door. Or like us, you could go to Cafe Trieste, which is just around the corner for an authentic and affordable Italian espresso. This is also where Francis Ford Copula wrote The Godfather. He even has his film production company just down the road. Stop number six is Chinatown, which is the oldest Chinatown in North America and is only a few minutes away. Here, we really enjoyed walking around and getting lost in the beautiful alleys. But there was one spot that we just couldn't miss, and this was the Golden Gate fortune cookie factory. Here you can see the process of having your own fortune cookie made, and you can even write a message to put inside them. It was super cheap, and they were absolutely delicious. It was actually one of the highlights of our trip. After that, we continued our exploration of Chinatown, and actually, you could spend hours here and see all the beautiful streets, temples, and the iconic Dragons Gate. Stop number seven on our list is the Palace of Fine Arts, which is another San Francisco institution that's been there for over a hundred years. It's a monumental structure originally built for the 1915 International Exposition to exhibit works of art. It's the perfect Instagram spot with photo shoots happening around every corner, but it's still a very peaceful and beautiful haven amongst the hustle and bustle of the city. Of course, you can't go to San Francisco without seeing the Golden Gate Bridge. Now, look, anyway you decide to see it, it's going to be beautiful, but these are our top three favorite ways to view it. The first one is Fort Point National Historic Site, where you can see the bridge from below. This is also where Hitchcock shot that very famous scene from Vertigo. And here you can really grasp the size of the bridge while hearing the sounds of the waves. This was definitely the most dramatic way to view the bridge. On selected days, you can explore the fort and the welcome center. Our next recommendation is crossing the bridge itself, which can either be done by car, on foot, or by bike. We decided to drive, which was thrilling, but it was also very hard to take everything in as it's over very quickly. However, it was still an amazing experience. At the time of filming, it was around a $10 toll, and this was driving into San Francisco. Our third favorite way to view the bridge was from the Golden Gate viewpoint where you can really get an idea of the scope of the bridge and you also get great views of the Bay Area and the city. If you want an even closer look at the bridge, you can go to Buttery Spencer, a half mile trail that starts from here. Our next recommendation is the Prescidio, which would literally take days to explore. But if you only have a couple of days just like us, there's still so much you can enjoy. The Prescidio is a historic park and former military base located on the northern edge of San Francisco, offering stunning views of the Golden Gate Bridge, lush forests, and scenic trails. It is now a vibrant cultural hub with museums, restaurants, and outdoor activities, blending natural beauty with rich history. We really enjoyed walking there. The parade ground is also home to the Walt Disney Family Museum. Unfortunately, it's only open a few days a week and was closed on the day we were there, so be sure to check the schedule beforehand if this stop is important for you. The museum was founded by Walt Disney's daughter and features the life and legacy of her father. We are big Disney fans, so we will definitely be back. But our Disney dreams were not completely destroyed as only a few minutes away is the home of Lucas Films. If you're a Star Wars fan, this is an absolute must. There's a beautiful Yoda fountain in the courtyard. And you can even peep through the doors of the lobby to see many Star Wars memorabilia, and who knows, maybe even bump into the man himself. And of course, last but not least, are San Francisco's famous cable cars. At the time of our visit, a day ticket was $8 and a day pass was $13. There are three main lines that climb all the way up San Francisco's famous hills. And it's a beautiful and iconic activity. They are manually operated, so we made sure to see the turnaround of the cable car, which happens at both the beginning and the end of each line. We chose to board at the intersection between Powell and Market Street, where we also got to see the turnaround. It was definitely a unique experience. The views were amazing, and the ride itself was thrilling, but we definitely recommend trying to be on the outside section as it's actually quite hard to experience the views properly from the inside. Some pro tips. Unless you consider the cable cars as a highlight of your trip, why not use them in a more productive way to get to a location that you're traveling to? Yes. And also, instead of uh waiting hours at the ticket booth, how about walking up a block and actually buy the ticket from the conductor of the train? We saw lots of people doing this and it actually saves lots of time. Also, if you're finished with your day pass, why don't you think about gifting it to someone else? Someone did that to us and it was a really lovely thing to do and it saved us lots of money. Yeah. And it was very kind. So that's it for our top 10 places to visit in San Francisco. But now the question some of you may have been waiting for. Is San Francisco safe? Ryan? Well, we did read some horror stories and we were a little apprehensive. The car break-ins are extremely common and even though we didn't witness this ourselves, warning signs were all over the city and it definitely put us off from driving, which is why we mainly walked or used Uber. The one time we did drive, we just made sure that we didn't leave anything visible in the car and fortunately we were fine. Another thing we heard was to avoid the tenderloin area. It's not a really touristic area, so we have no real reason to go. So, we decided not to. But also, we did hear is getting a bit better. We live in London, so we're used to always have to be careful and watch out for our bag and everything. But our main advice if you are in San Francisco is go there, have fun, and don't get too paranoid. We have another video of our top 10 day trips from San Francisco that you can take. Be sure to check it out. There is so much beauty just minutes outside of the city that you don't want to miss. Also, if you like this video, please like and subscribe. It goes a long way and it really means a lot. Stay tuned for all of our future videos where we have many trips and tips to share with you. We're always on an adventure and we cannot wait to take you with us. And now, see you down the road. [Music]
